'''[[Intentionality]] bias''', which is known as '''[[intention]] bias''' for short, is a [[bias]] that makes people believe that all human behavior is intentional and that unconscious and/or accidental behavior is less likely behavior. This [[cognitive bias]] can happen even if the evidence against the assumption is presented to the person. This tendency has extensive ramifications for [[societal dynamics]], [[interpersonal relationship]]s, as well as [[conflict resolution]]. Intentionality bias may result in misjudgment of people's behavior, misunderstanding as well as miscommunication. This tendency may contribute to unfavorable prejudices and [[stereotype]]s, escalate [[Conflict (process)|conflicts]] and [[Social stigma|stigmatize]] people.<ref>{{cite web|last=|first=|title=What is Intentionality Bias In Behavioral Economics?|website=thebehavioralscientist.com|publisher=|date=9 July 2023|url=https://www.thebehavioralscientist.com/glossary/intentionality-bias|access-date=November 1, 2024}}</ref>

==Definition==
Forming judgements about '''intentions''' is a cornerstone of human social functioning, as they influence how we perceive, respond to, and predict other people's behaviour.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Baldwin |first1=Dare A. |last2=Baird |first2=Jodie A. |date=April 2001 |title=Discerning intentions in dynamic human action |journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences |language=en |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=171–178 |doi= 10.1016/S1364-6613(00)01615-6 |pmid=11287271 }}</ref> A series of psychological studies demonstrated that '''adults''' implicitly and automatically gravitate towards intentional explanations of actions, a phenomenon termed '<nowiki />'''intentionality bias'''<nowiki />'.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Rosset |first1=Evelyn |date= September 2008 |title=It's no accident: Our bias for intentional explanations |journal=Cognition |language=en |volume=108 |issue=3 |pages=771–780 |doi= 10.1016/j.cognition.2008.07.001 |pmid=18692779 }}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Moore |first1=J.W. | last2=Pope | first2=A. |date= November 2014 |title=The intentionality bias and schizotypy |journal=Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology |language=en |volume=67 |issue=11 |pages=2218–2224 |doi= 10.1080/17470218.2014.911332 |pmid=24712872 }}</ref> Interestingly, this tendency was also documented in '''children''' when they believed that natural events were created intentionally even by a non-human agent, and they tended to attribute intentions to artifacts.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kelemen |first1=Deborah|date= May 2004 |title=Are Children "Intuitive Theists"?: Reasoning About Purpose and Design in Nature |journal=Psychological Science |language=en |volume=15 |issue=5 |pages=295–301 |doi= 10.1111/j.0956-7976.2004.00672.x |pmid=15102137 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Preissler |first1=Melissa Allen | last2=Bloom | first2=Paul |date= January 2008 |title= Two-year-olds use artist intention to understand drawings |journal=Cognition |language=en |volume=106 |issue=1 |pages=512–518 |doi= 10.1016/j.cognition.2007.02.002 |pmid=17391660 }}</ref> 

==Measurement==
In her seminal work, '''Rosset (2008)''' proposed a paradigm for examining intentionality bias, which later laid the methodological foundation of this research area. In Rosset's experiments, participants were presented with blocks of sentences, each of which contained several test and control sentences. While ''test sentences'' described behaviours with ambiguous intentions, ''control sentences'' described either unambiguously deliberate or unambiguously accidental actions. These sentences were shown consecutively and participants judged if each action was done ''"on purpose"'' or ''"by accident"''. 

However, the author admitted a fundamental flaw in her paradigm which may undermine the internal validity of findings: written sentences may reflect a '''linguistic bias''' rather than intentionality bias, as different wording may imply different levels of intentions.<ref name=":0" /> 

'''Moore and Pope (2014)''' built on this limitation and developed a novel procedure that utilises visual stimuli to investigate intentionality bias. In their study, participants were presented with ''short video clips'' depicting ambiguous movements, then verbally reported whether they believed each movement was performed intentionally or accidentally. This refinement thus allowed researchers to accurately capture intentionality bias.<ref name=":1" /> 

==Explanations==
One explanation for intentionality bias is the dual-process model of intention attribution. '''Rosset (2008)''' suggested that intentional judgements are made automatically and nonconsciously due to intentionality bias, thus serving as a '''cognitive default'''. This involuntary response can be overridden or inhibited by higher levels of processing, which develop with age and knowledge of alternative causes and social norms. Moreover, participants were more likely to attribute intentions to prototypically accidental actions, and they recalled more unintentional sentences than intentional ones, indicating that making accidental judgements is cognitively expensive and requires more processing.<ref name=":0" />  

The importance of higher cognitive processes in overriding intentionality bias is further highlighted in a study of '''anger''' and its impact on intentional inferences. Angry individuals were found to make more intentional judgements compared to non-angry participants, possibly due to their greater reliance on superficial cognitive processing.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Subra |first1=Baptiste |date= April 2021 |title=The effect of anger on intentionality bias |journal= Aggressive Behaviour |language=en |volume=47 |issue=4 |pages=464–471 |doi= 10.1002/ab.21964 |pmid=33792921 |url=https://hal.science/hal-03464292 }}</ref> 

As an extension from the dual-process model, '''impaired inhibitory control''' was put forward as an underlying mechanism of intentionality bias. Research revealed that '''schizophrenic''' patients showed a significant tendency to over-attribute intentions to actions compared to healthy participants, thus suggesting a link between inhibitory processes and intentionality bias.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Peyroux |first1=Elodie | last2 = Strickland | first2 = Brent | last3=Tapiero | first3 = Isabelle | last4=Franck | first4 = Nicolas |date= November 2014 |title=The intentionality bias in schizophrenia |journal=Psychiatry Research |language=en |volume=219 |issue=3 |pages=426–430 |doi= 10.1016/j.psychres.2014.06.034 |pmid=25042425 }}</ref> Furthermore, '''alcohol''' has been shown to reduce inhibitory control, and intoxicated individuals were more likely to interpret actions as deliberate compared to sober people.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Fillmore |first1=Mark T. |date= September 2003 |title=Drug Abuse as a Problem of Impaired Control: Current Approaches and Findings |journal= Behavioral and Cognitive Neuroscience Reviews |language=en |volume=2 |issue=3 |pages=179–197 |doi= 10.1177/1534582303257007 |pmid=15006292 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bègue |first1=Laurent | last2=Bushman | first2=Brad J. | last3=Giancola | first3=Peter R. | last4 = Subra | first4=Baptiste | last5=Rosset | first5=Evelyn |date= September 2010 |title='There is no such thing as an accident,' especially when people are drunk |journal= Personality & Social Psychology Bulletin |language=en |volume=36 |issue=10 |pages=1301–1304 |doi= 10.1177/0146167210383044 |pmid=20833796 }}</ref> Moreover, '''time pressure''' was shown to reduce self-regulatory abilities, and under time constraints, participants made more intentional judgements since they may not have accessed resources to inhibit automatic responses.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wegner |first1=D.M. | last2=Erber | first2=R. |date= 1992 |title=The hyperaccessibility of suppressed thoughts |journal= Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=63 |issue=6 |pages=903–912 |doi= 10.1037/0022-3514.63.6.903}}</ref>   

Another possible explanation for intentionality bias stems from the '''error management theory of cognitive biases''', which states that cognitive biases emerge when the costs of false-positive and false-negative errors are unbalanced.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Haselton |first1=M.G. | last2=Buss | first2=D.M. |date= 2000 |title=Error management theory: A new perspective on biases in cross-sex mind reading |journal= Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=78 |issue=1 |pages=81–91 |doi= 10.1037/0022-3514.78.1.81 |pmid=10653507 |bibcode=2000JPSP...78...81H }}</ref> According to '''Moore and Pope (2014)''', it may be more beneficial to attribute intentionality to others' behaviour, because a ''false-positive'' error (i.e. believing an action is intentional when it is accidental) is less detrimental than a ''false-negative'' error (i.e. believing an action is accidental when it is intentional). Consequently, intentionality bias prompts individuals to be alert to the involvement of other agents who may be a threat, making it an '''adaptive''' cognitive response.<ref name=":1" />

==Individual differences==
The magnitude of intentionality bias may vary individually. Findings suggested that individuals with higher '''cognitive empathy''' (i.e. the ability to understand people's emotions) and better '''perspective-taking skills''' were more prone to making intentional judgements. Conversely, affective empathy (i.e. the ability to vicariously experience people's emotions) was not a reliable predictor of intention inferences. This suggested that cognitively empathetic individuals may have an overly sensitive system of intention attribution.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Slavny |first1=Rachel J.M. | last2=Moore | first2=James W. |date= February 2018 |title=Individual differences in the intentionality bias and its association with cognitive empathy |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |language=en |volume=122 |pages=104–108 |doi= 10.1016/j.paid.2017.10.010}}</ref> 

Research also revealed that the extent of intentionality bias was associated with individual differences in '''schizotypy''' (i.e. schizophrenia-like symptoms found in healthy people). Participants who scored higher on the schizotypy rating scales tended to exhibit stronger bias compared to those who scored lower.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, '''social actions''' were more likely to be judged as intentional compared to non-social events among individuals with schizophrenia-like traits, thus strengthening the link between intentionality bias and deficits in inhibitory control.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Roodenrys |first1=Steven| last2=Barkus | first2=Emma | last3=Woolrych | first3=Tracey J. | last4=Miller | first4=Leonie M. | last5=Favelle | first5=Simone K. |date= January 2021 |title=The intentionality bias in schizotypy: a social matter |journal=Cognitive Neuropsychiatry |language=en |volume=26 |issue=1 |pages=55–72 |doi= 10.1080/13546805.2020.1865894 |pmid=33403932 }}</ref> 

==Implications==
Research into intentionality bias suggested that humans are inclined towards intentional judgements, which had significant implications for the understanding of everyday situations.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rosset |first1=Evelyn | last2=Rottman | first2=Joshua |date= January 2014 |title=The Big 'Whoops!' in the Study of Intentional Behavior: An Appeal for a New Framework in Understanding Human Actions |journal=Journal of Cognition and Culture |language=en |volume=14 |issue=1–2 |pages=27–39 |doi= 10.1163/15685373-12342108}}</ref> For instance, intentionality bias was associated with '''aggressive behaviour''', and aggressive children were more likely to attribute hostile intentions to a peer.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=De Castro |first1=Bram Orobio | last2=Veerman | first2=Jan W.| last3=Koops | first3=Willem | last4=Bosch | first4=Joop D.| last5=Monshouwer | first5=Heidi J. |date= June 2002 |title=Hostile Attribution of Intent and Aggressive Behavior: A Meta-Analysis |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=73 |issue=3 |pages=916–934 |doi= 10.1111/1467-8624.00447 |pmid=12038560 }}</ref> Moreover, intentional inferences are often accompanied by '''accountability''' attribution, and individuals were more motivated to blame intentional harm-doers.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Malle |first1=Bertram F.| last2=Knobe | first2=Joshua |date= March 1997 |title=The Folk Concept of Intentionality |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |language=en |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=101–121 |doi= 10.1006/jesp.1996.1314}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ames |first1=Daniel L.| last2=Fiske | first2=Susan T. |date= March 2015 |title=Perceived intent motivates people to magnify observed harms |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=112 |issue=12 |pages=3599–3605 |doi= 10.1073/pnas.1501592112 |doi-access=free |pmid=25733850 |pmc=4378403 |bibcode=2015PNAS..112.3599A }}</ref> 

On a societal level, judgements of intentionality are especially crucial in '''law enforcement''', where the decision of whether someone is guilty or innocent is contingent on how intentional their actions were. For example, when a killing is shown to be intentional, a defendant may be accused of murder rather than manslaughter and receive more severe punishment. Therefore, intentionality bias highlights the importance of the rule that assumes people are innocent by default until proven otherwise.<ref name=":0" /> 

Furthermore, a link between the endorsement of '''conspiracy theories''' and intentionality bias has been discovered. '''Brotherton and French (2015)''' showed that individuals with stronger bias towards intentional explanations of behaviour were more likely to find conspiracy theories plausible.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Brotherton |first1=Robert| last2=French | first2=Christopher C. |date= May 2015 |title=Intention Seekers: Conspiracist Ideation and Biased Attributions of Intentionality|journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=10 |issue=5 |article-number=e0124125 |doi= 10.1371/journal.pone.0124125 |doi-access=free |pmid=25970175 |pmc=4430300 |bibcode=2015PLoSO..1024125B }}</ref> Thus, investigations into intentionality bias may illuminate the spread and mechanism of conspiracy theories. 

==Limitations==
However, there is some counter-evidence that challenges the effect of the intentionality bias. A '''replication study''' failed to reproduce the results of '''Rosset (2008)''', and findings revealed that participants were not more likely to make intentional judgements in a speeded compared to an unspeeded condition. Moreover, participants made unintentional judgements faster and more consistently than intentional ones, which contradicted the predictions of intentionality bias.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hughes |first1=Jamie S. | last2=Sandry | first2=Joshua | last3=Trafimow | first3=David |date= 2012 |title=Intentional Inferences Are Not More Likely Than Unintentional Ones: Some Evidence Against the Intentionality Bias Hypothesis |journal=The Journal of Social Psychology |language=en |volume=152 |issue=1 |pages=1–4 |doi= 10.1080/00224545.2011.565383 |pmid=22308757 }}</ref> 

Furthermore, Rosset's dual-process model of intention attribution may be oversimplistic or incomplete. '''Eisenkoeck et al. (2024)''' found that contrary to Rosset's predictions that higher cognitive load would increase intentional judgements due to insufficient resources inhibiting automatic responses, participants with increased ''working memory'' cognitive load were not more susceptible to intentionality bias.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Eisenkoeck |first1=A.E. | last2=de Fockert | first2=J.W. | last3=Moore | first3=J.W. |date= November 2024 |title=Investigating the effect of cognitive load on the intentionality bias |journal=Psychological Research |language=en |volume=89 |issue=1 |article-number=4 |doi= 10.1007/s00426-024-02047-3 |pmid=39531079 |pmc=11557632 }}</ref> This evidence suggests that making intentional inferences may be more complex than a simple two-process model.

==References==
{{Reflist}}
